title: Moist Air & Air Conditioning (Psychrometrics)
summary: Treat air as dry air + water vapor. Master $\omega$, $\varphi$, dew point, wet-bulb and the enthalpy $h=c_{p,a}t+\omega(r_0+c_{p,v}t)$; read the psychrometric chart; and solve the five AC processes (heating, cooling, dehumidification, humidification, mixing) with mass/energy balances on the conserved dry air.
objectives: Compute $p_v$, $p_a$, $\omega$, $\varphi$, dew point and enthalpy of a moist-air state from $p$, $t$, and one humidity measure
Explain and use wet-bulb / dry-bulb temperatures and the constant-enthalpy adiabatic-saturation idea
Locate and move states on the psychrometric (Mollier/Carrier) chart for each process
Write dry-air, water and energy balances for steady-flow AC devices
Solve sensible heating/cooling, cooling-with-dehumidification, steam and adiabatic humidification, and adiabatic mixing numerically
Split a cooling+dehumidification duty into sensible and latent parts
Describe the summer and winter air-conditioning process trains

blocks.text: What moist air is

**Moist air** is just a mixture of two ideal gases: **dry air** and **water vapor**. The water vapor is a tiny fraction by mass, but it controls comfort (how 'sticky' or 'dry' the air feels) and it is the whole point of air-conditioning.

By **Dalton's law** the total pressure is the sum of partial pressures:
$$p = p_a + p_v$$
where $p_a$ is the dry-air partial pressure and $p_v$ is the water-vapor partial pressure. In all chart work we take $p = 1\text{ bar} = 101325\text{ Pa}$ (atmospheric).

Two gas constants you will reuse constantly:
- Dry air: $R_a = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$ (molar mass $\approx 28.97$)
- Water vapor: $R_v = 461.5\ \text{J/kg·K}$ (molar mass $18$)

Their ratio gives the famous $0.622 = R_a/R_v = 18/28.97$ that appears in the humidity ratio.

Mass of water vapor per **kg of dry air** — NOT per kg of mixture. This is the key trick of psychrometrics: we always normalize per unit of dry air, because the dry air is conserved while the vapor changes.
$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a}\quad[\text{kg}_v/\text{kg}_a]$$

blocks.caption: Humidity ratio from partial pressures. Since $p_v\ll p$, $\omega$ is usually a few grams per kg.

blocks.text:
Ratio of the actual vapor partial pressure to the saturation pressure at the same temperature:
$$\varphi = \frac{p_v}{p_s(T)}$$
$p_s(T)$ is read from the **water saturation tables** (e.g. $p_s(20^\circ C)=0.02339$ bar). $\varphi=100\%$ means the air is saturated and cannot hold more vapor.

blocks.caption: Combine $p_v=\varphi p_s$ with the definition of $\omega$. This is the everyday working formula.

blocks.title: φ is NOT ω
blocks.text: Two parcels at the same $\varphi=50\%$ but different temperatures have **different** $\omega$, because $p_s$ rises steeply with $T$. Warm 50% air holds far more water than cold 50% air. Heating air at constant $\omega$ **lowers** $\varphi$; cooling it **raises** $\varphi$.
Specific enthalpy of moist air

We measure enthalpy **per kg of dry air** (the '$1+\omega$' basis: one kg of dry air carrying $\omega$ kg of vapor). Reference states: dry air $h=0$ at $0^\circ C$; water vapor $h=0$ for saturated liquid at $0^\circ C$, so the vapor carries the latent heat $r_0$.

blocks.caption: with $c_{p,a}=1.006$, $c_{p,v}=1.875$ kJ/kg·K, $r_0=2500$ kJ/kg. The term $c_{p,a}t$ is **sensible** (temperature); $\omega r_0$ is **latent** (the water content).

blocks.title: t must be in °C here
blocks.text: In $h=c_{p,a}t+\omega(r_0+c_{p,v}t)$ the temperature is **Celsius**, because the reference is $0^\circ C$. This is the one place you do NOT convert to Kelvin. (You DO use Kelvin in $v_a=R_aT/p_a$.)

blocks.title: Worked: state from p, t, φ (Ex 5.1)

blocks.text: Dew point, wet bulb, dry bulb

Cool the air at **constant $\omega$** (constant $p_v$). The dew point is the temperature at which $\varphi$ reaches 100% and the first droplet condenses. So $T_D$ is the **saturation temperature corresponding to the actual $p_v$**: $p_s(T_D)=p_v$. Read it backward from the steam tables.

**Dry-bulb** temperature is the ordinary thermometer reading. **Wet-bulb** temperature comes from a thermometer with a wet wick: evaporation cools it. The drier the air, the more evaporation, the lower the wet-bulb, so the gap $t_{db}-t_{wb}$ measures humidity.

Key idealization: the wet-bulb (adiabatic-saturation) process is at nearly **constant enthalpy**, $h_{db}\approx h_{wb}$. At the wet-bulb state the air is saturated ($\varphi=100\%$).

blocks.title: Find φ from wet & dry bulb (Ex 5.4)

steps.label: Back out φ
steps.text: Invert $\omega=0.622\varphi p_s/(p-\varphi p_s)$ at $t_{db}$ to get $\varphi_{db}=\dfrac{\omega_{db}\,p}{p_s(0.622+\omega_{db})}$. For $t_{db}=30,\,t_{wb}=27^\circ C$ this gives $\varphi\approx80\%$.

blocks.text: Quick check on dew point.

question.prompt: Air at $20^\circ C$, $\varphi=50\%$. Its dew point is the temperature where:
question.explanation: Cooling at constant $p_v$ reaches saturation when $p_s$ falls to equal the actual $p_v$; that temperature is the dew point. A confuses total with partial pressure. C: $\varphi$ rises to 100% on cooling, not back to 50%. D: $\omega$ stays constant during the cooling-to-dew-point, it does not change until condensation starts.
question.theory: Dew point: $p_s(T_D)=p_v=\varphi p_s(T)$. Cooling at constant $\omega$ raises $\varphi$ toward 100%.

blocks.text: Reading the psychrometric chart

The **Mollier/Carrier chart** plots all of this for $p=1$ bar. Axes/lines:
- **horizontal-ish: dry-bulb temperature $t$**; **vertical: humidity ratio $\omega$** (right side).
- curved lines of constant **$\varphi$**; the top curve is $\varphi=100\%$ (saturation).
- straight slanted lines of constant **$h$** (≈ constant wet-bulb).
- a point is fixed by any **two** independent properties (Gibbs rule gives 3 variables, one is fixed by $p$).

On the chart the four basic processes are simple moves:
- **Sensible heating** → move right (↑t) at constant $\omega$.
- **Sensible cooling** → move left at constant $\omega$ (until you hit $\varphi=100\%$).
- **Cooling + dehumidification** → down the saturation curve (vapor condenses).
- **Humidification** → move up (↑ω); adiabatic water-spray is along constant $h$.

blocks.title: Psychrometric chart explorer
blocks.caption: Drag a state point and watch $\varphi$, $\omega$, dew point, wet-bulb and $h$ update. Trace heating (horizontal), cooling-to-saturation, and constant-$h$ humidification to see how each process moves on the chart.

blocks.text: Mass & energy balances on the dry air

Every device is steady-state. The **dry-air mass flow is constant** through it: $\dot m_{a1}=\dot m_{a2}=\dot m_a$. Water vapor can be added/removed:
$$\dot m_w = \dot m_a(\omega_2-\omega_1)$$
($\dot m_w>0$ added by humidification, $<0$ removed by condensation).

blocks.caption: Energy balance (no shaft work, $\Delta ke=\Delta pe=0$). The liquid term $\dot m_w h_w$ is small and often dropped for cooling coils.

blocks.title: Get ṁ_a from volume flow
blocks.text: Problems give a volumetric flow $\dot V$ at the inlet. Use the dry-air specific volume $v_a=R_aT/p_a$ (T in **K**!) and $\dot m_a\approx \dot V/v_a$, since moist-air density $\approx$ dry-air density. E.g. $25^\circ C$, $p_a=0.978$ bar → $v_a=0.875\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$.
The five air-conditioning processes

Pass air over a hot/cold coil **above the dew point** → only temperature changes, $\omega_1=\omega_2$:
$$\dot Q = \dot m_a(h_2-h_1)=\dot m_a(c_{p,a}+\omega c_{p,v})(t_2-t_1)$$
Heating $\dot Q>0$, cooling $\dot Q<0$.

Cool **below the dew point**: vapor condenses on the coil, $\omega$ drops. The exit (ideal) sits on $\varphi=100\%$. Condensate: $\dot m_w=\dot m_a(\omega_1-\omega_2)$. The duty splits into a **sensible** part (Δt) and a **latent** part (Δω) — that split is the favourite exam sub-question.

Add water to raise $\omega$. **With steam**: $\omega$ and $t$ both rise. **With liquid water (adiabatic air washer / evaporative cooling)**: the process is ≈ constant $h$, so the air **cools** as it humidifies (the spray evaporates using the air's own sensible heat). $\dot m_w=\dot m_a(\omega_2-\omega_1)$.

Two moist-air streams merge with no heat loss. Dry-air, vapor and energy balances give the lever rule:
$$\frac{\dot m_{a1}}{\dot m_{a2}}=\frac{\omega_3-\omega_2}{\omega_1-\omega_3}=\frac{h_3-h_2}{h_1-h_3}$$
The mix state 3 lies on the straight line joining 1 and 2, divided by the mass ratio.

blocks.title: Evaporative cooling intuition
blocks.text: Adiabatic water spray = **free cooling in dry climates**. Air gives up sensible heat ($t\downarrow$) to evaporate water ($\omega\uparrow$) at $h\approx$ const. It only works when the air is dry (large $t_{db}-t_{wb}$); in humid air there is little wet-bulb depression and almost no cooling.

blocks.title: Worked: cooling + dehumidification (Ex 5.9)
blocks.text:
**Given** $\dot V=7500\ \text{m}^3/h$, $t_1=25^\circ C$, $\varphi_1=70\%$; cool to saturation, then post-heat to $t_3=20^\circ C$, $\varphi_3=40\%$.

- State 1: $p_{s1}=0.03169$ bar → $p_{v1}=0.02218$, $p_{a1}=0.978$ bar. $v_a=R_aT_1/p_{a1}=0.875\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, so $\dot m_a=\dot V/(3600\,v_a)\approx2.38\ \text{kg/s}$. $\omega_1=0.0141$, $h_1=61.1$ kJ/kg.
- State 3: $\omega_3=0.00588$, $h_3=35.0$ kJ/kg. Post-heat is at constant $\omega$, so $\omega_2=\omega_3=0.00588$.
- State 2 (saturated): $\varphi_2=100\%$ with that $\omega_2$ → $t_2\approx6^\circ C$, $h_2=20.8$ kJ/kg.
- Condensate $\dot m_w=\dot m_a(\omega_1-\omega_2)=2.38(0.0141-0.00588)=0.0196\ \text{kg/s}$.
- $\dot Q_{cool}=\dot m_a(h_2-h_1)+\dot m_w h_{l}\approx 2.38(20.8-61.1)+0.0196\cdot25.2=-95.3\ \text{kW}$.
- $\dot Q_{heat}=\dot m_a(h_3-h_2)=2.38(35.0-20.8)=33.9\ \text{kW}$.

**Answer:** $\dot Q_{cool}=-95.3$ kW, $\dot m_w=0.0196$ kg/s, $\dot Q_{heat}=33.9$ kW.

blocks.title: Sensible/latent split via point K
blocks.text: To split a cooling+dehumidification duty, define auxiliary point **K** at the start temperature and the end humidity. The **latent** part is the constant-temperature leg (change in $\omega$) and the **sensible** part is the constant-$\omega$ leg (change in $t$). Draw the right-angle on the chart so you don't swap them: e.g. in Ex 5.12, $\dot Q_L=\dot m_a(h_K-h_A)$ and $\dot Q_S=\dot m_a(h_B-h_K)$.
Process identification.

question.prompt: Liquid water is sprayed into a warm dry air stream with no heat added (adiabatic air washer). What happens?
question.explanation: Evaporating liquid uses the air's own sensible heat, so $t$ drops while $\omega$ rises at nearly constant enthalpy (evaporative cooling). A describes steam injection (adds energy, $h$↑). C is wrong: spraying water adds vapor, $\omega$ rises, and $\varphi$ rises toward saturation. D ignores the temperature drop — only true if you also added exactly the evaporation heat.
question.theory: Adiabatic humidification with liquid water: $h\approx$ const, $\omega\uparrow$, $t\downarrow$. Steam humidification adds energy so $h\uparrow$.

blocks.title: General recipe for any moist-air problem

steps.label: 1. Tables
steps.text: For each state read $p_s(t)$ from the steam saturation tables.

steps.label: 3. Dry-air flow
steps.text: $v_a=R_aT/p_a$ (T in K), $\dot m_a=\dot V/v_a$. Dry air is conserved through the device.

steps.label: 4. Balances
steps.text: Water: $\dot m_w=\dot m_a(\omega_2-\omega_1)$. Energy: $\dot Q=\dot m_a(h_2-h_1)\pm\dot m_w h_w$.

steps.label: 5. Identify the leg
steps.text: Heating/cooling above dew pt: $\omega$ const. Dehumidify: end on $\varphi=100\%$. Adiabatic spray/mixing: $h$ (≈) const. Steam: line of slope $h_v$.

practice.prompt: The humidity ratio $\omega$ is defined as the mass of water vapor per:

options.content: kg of saturated vapor at that $T$

practice.explanation: $\omega=m_v/m_a$, per kg of **dry air**, because dry air is the conserved quantity. A would be mass fraction, not $\omega$. C is the basis for $\varphi$, not $\omega$. D mixes mass and mole bases.

practice.prompt: Air at $1$ bar, $20^\circ C$, $\varphi=50\%$. With $p_s(20^\circ C)=0.02339$ bar, the humidity ratio is closest to:
practice.explanation: $p_v=0.5\times0.02339=0.0117$ bar; $\omega=0.622\cdot0.0117/(1-0.0117)=0.00736$. A used $\varphi=100\%$. B used $0.622\cdot p_v/p$ without the factor and a wrong $\varphi$. D dropped the $0.622$ factor entirely.

practice.prompt: Why are the two axes of the Mollier chart drawn non-orthogonal (the $\omega$ axis is inclined)?

options.content: To spread out the constant-$t$ lines, which would otherwise be nearly parallel and crowded
Because $p_v$ is negative for cold air
To make enthalpy numerically equal temperature

practice.explanation: $\partial h/\partial\omega|_t=r_0+c_{p,v}t\approx2500$ is huge, so isotherms are almost parallel and squeezed; inclining the $\omega$ axis fans them out so the chart is readable. A is false — constant-$\varphi$ lines are curved. C is nonsense; $p_v>0$. D confuses two different quantities.
practice.theory: Isotherm slope $=r_0+c_{p,v}t$; the skewed axes amplify the region of interest.
practice.source: Lecture Part 5, drawing the Mollier diagram

practice.prompt: A sensible heating coil warms $\dot m_a=2\ \text{kg}_a/s$ of air with $\omega=0.008$ from $10$ to $30^\circ C$. Using $\dot Q=\dot m_a(c_{p,a}+\omega c_{p,v})\Delta t$, the heat rate is about:
practice.explanation: $(c_{p,a}+\omega c_{p,v})=1.006+0.008\cdot1.875=1.021$; $\dot Q=2\cdot1.021\cdot20=40.8\ \text{kW}$. B used $\dot m_a=1$ instead of 2. C doubled $\Delta t$ wrongly. D used a $c_v$-type value ~0.72 instead of $c_{p,a}$.
practice.theory: Sensible-only heating at constant $\omega$: $\dot Q=\dot m_a(c_{p,a}+\omega c_{p,v})(t_2-t_1)$.

practice.prompt: Moist air is cooled from $25^\circ C$, $\varphi=70\%$ (dew point $\approx19^\circ C$) down to $5^\circ C$. What happens to $\omega$?

options.content: $\omega$ is unchanged because cooling is sensible
$\omega$ decreases because vapor condenses below the dew point

practice.explanation: Below the dew point ($19^\circ C$) the air is saturated and further cooling condenses vapor, so $\omega$ falls (here from 0.0141 to 0.0055). A is true only above the dew point. B is backwards — cooling never adds vapor. D is invented.
practice.theory: Cooling at constant $\omega$ until dew point; below it, ride the $\varphi=100\%$ curve as $\omega$ drops.

practice.prompt: In the moist-air enthalpy formula $h=c_{p,a}t+\omega(r_0+c_{p,v}t)$, the temperature $t$ must be in:

options.content: either, the result is the same

practice.explanation: The reference states are at $0^\circ C$, so $t$ is in Celsius. A/D would add ~273 to every term and ruin the value. C is false because the formula is linear with a non-zero reference, so units matter.
practice.theory: Enthalpy reference: dry air $h=0$ at $0^\circ C$, vapor $h=0$ for sat. liquid at $0^\circ C$ → t in °C.

practice.prompt: Two streams mix adiabatically: stream 1 ($\dot m_{a1}=19.8\ \text{kg}_a/s$, $h_1=61.1$) and stream 2 ($\dot m_{a2}=34.9\ \text{kg}_a/s$, $h_2=23.2$ kJ/kg). The mixed enthalpy $h_3$ is about:
practice.explanation: $h_3=(\dot m_{a1}h_1+\dot m_{a2}h_2)/(\dot m_{a1}+\dot m_{a2})=(19.8\cdot61.1+34.9\cdot23.2)/54.7=36.9$. A is the unweighted average $(h_1+h_2)/2$. C added the enthalpies. D weighted by the wrong (swapped) masses.
practice.theory: Adiabatic mixing: $h_3=\sum\dot m_{ai}h_i/\sum\dot m_{ai}$; state 3 on the 1–2 line by the lever rule.

practice.prompt: To get the dry-air mass flow from a volumetric flow, you use $v_a=R_aT/p_a$. Here $T$ and $p_a$ must be:
practice.explanation: Ideal-gas law needs absolute temperature (K), and the dry-air partial pressure is $p_a=p-p_v$. B uses total pressure and °C (both wrong). C uses the vapor pressure instead of dry-air pressure. D uses °C in the gas law, which is wrong.

practice.prompt: Adiabatic spray humidification of $1\ \text{kg}_a/s$ air: $40^\circ C$, $\varphi=20\%$ ($\omega_1=0.0093$, $h_1=64.24$) cooled to $30^\circ C$ at constant $h$. The water added $\dot m_w$ is about:
practice.explanation: At constant $h$: $\omega_2=(h_1-c_{p,a}t_2)/(r_0+c_{p,v}t_2)=(64.24-30.18)/2556.3=0.0133$; $\dot m_w=\omega_2-\omega_1=0.0133-0.0093=0.0040$. A is $\omega_1+\omega_2$. C is $\omega_2$ itself (forgot to subtract $\omega_1$). D is $\omega_1$.
practice.theory: Adiabatic liquid humidification: $h_2\approx h_1$, $\dot m_w=\dot m_a(\omega_2-\omega_1)$.

practice.prompt: For a cooling+dehumidification process, the **latent** rate of heat transfer is associated with:

options.content: the change in temperature at constant $\omega$
the change in $\omega$ at constant temperature
the pressure drop across the coil

practice.explanation: Latent load = change in moisture (Δω) at constant t; sensible load = change in t at constant ω. A is the definition of sensible, not latent. C and D are negligible/irrelevant in these balances.
practice.theory: $\dot Q_{lat}\propto\Delta\omega$ (constant t leg), $\dot Q_{sens}\propto\Delta t$ (constant ω leg); split via point K.
practice.source: Lecture Part 5, sensible and latent loads

practice.prompt: On the psychrometric chart, pure sensible heating moves the state point:

options.content: horizontally to the right (higher t, same ω)

practice.explanation: Heating adds no water, so $\omega$ is constant and only $t$ rises → move right. A is dehumidification. C is adiabatic humidification (evaporative cooling). D is humidification at constant temperature (steam-ish).
practice.theory: Heating/cooling above dew point: $\omega$ const, horizontal move; $\varphi$ falls on heating.

practice.prompt: Heating a 20 kg/s dry-air stream ($t_1=20^\circ C$, $h_1=34.9$) with $\dot Q=29.24$ kW AND injecting $\dot m_v=0.0305$ kg/s of saturated steam ($h_g=2675$ kJ/kg). The exit enthalpy $h_2$ is about:
practice.explanation: $h_2=h_1+(\dot Q+\dot m_v h_g)/\dot m_a=34.9+(29.24+0.0305\cdot2675)/20=34.9+(29.24+81.6)/20=34.9+5.54=40.5$. B dropped the steam-enthalpy term. C included only $\dot Q$. D double-counted by adding $h_g/1000$ wrongly.
practice.theory: Energy balance with steam: $\dot m_a h_1+\dot Q+\dot m_v h_g=\dot m_a h_2$.

options.content: higher than the dry-bulb (except at saturation)
lower than (or equal to) the dry-bulb, the gap growing as air gets drier

practice.explanation: Evaporation cools the wet wick, so $t_{wb}\le t_{db}$, with the depression $t_{db}-t_{wb}$ larger for drier air. A reverses the inequality. B is false: in unsaturated air $t_{dew}<t_{wb}<t_{db}$. D is false — the whole point is that $t_{wb}$ measures humidity.
practice.theory: Adiabatic saturation: $h_{db}\approx h_{wb}$; $t_{dew}\le t_{wb}\le t_{db}$, equal only at $\varphi=100\%$.

practice.prompt: In a winter system, return air (E) and outdoor air (R) mix at M, then are heated to injection state I, with $h_M=[(\dot m_{aI}-\dot m_{aR})h_E+\dot m_{aR}h_R]/\dot m_{aI}$. If all air is recirculated ($\dot m_{aR}=0$), then $h_M$ equals:
practice.explanation: With $\dot m_{aR}=0$ the formula collapses to $h_M=h_E$: no fresh air, so the mix is just the return air. A would need all fresh air. C is the equal-mass average, not this case. D is meaningless.
practice.theory: Mixing point: $h_M=[(\dot m_{aI}-\dot m_{aR})h_E+\dot m_{aR}h_R]/\dot m_{aI}$; recirc fraction sets where M sits.

practice.prompt: Relative humidity $\varphi$ during a pure heating process (constant $\omega$):

options.content: increases, because warm air holds more water
decreases, because $p_s$ rises while $p_v$ stays fixed

practice.explanation: $\varphi=p_v/p_s(T)$. Heating keeps $p_v$ (hence $\omega$) fixed but raises $p_s$, so $\varphi$ falls. That is why winter heating makes indoor air feel dry. A confuses capacity with $\varphi$. C is wrong — only $\omega$ is constant. D happens on cooling to saturation, the opposite.

exam.title: State properties of moist air
exam.statement: Moist air at $p=1$ bar, $t=28^\circ C$ has relative humidity $\varphi=80\%$. Determine (1) the dry-air partial pressure, (2) the vapor partial pressure, (3) the humidity ratio, (4) the specific enthalpy. Table: $p_s(28^\circ C)=0.03782$ bar.
exam.tips: Keep $t$ in °C for $h$. The latent term $\omega r_0\approx48.5$ kJ/kg dominates the sensible term — a sanity check on size.

exam.title: Adiabatic mixing of two streams
exam.statement: Two moist-air streams at 1 bar mix adiabatically. Stream 1: $\dot m_1=20$ kg/s, $30^\circ C$, $\varphi_1=45\%$. Stream 2: $\dot m_2=35$ kg/s, $15^\circ C$, $\varphi_2=30\%$. Find the mixture enthalpy $h_3$ and temperature $t_3$. Tables: $p_s(30)=0.04246$, $p_s(15)=0.01706$ bar.
exam.given: $\dot m_1,\dot m_2$ are total moist-air flows; need dry-air flows. $c_{p,a}=1.006$, $c_{p,v}=1.875$, $r_0=2500$.
exam.tips: Convert the given total flows to dry-air flows before averaging. State 3 lies on the chart line 1–2, closer to stream 2 (larger mass).

exam.title: Cooling + dehumidification + post-heating
exam.statement: $\dot V=7500\ \text{m}^3/h$ of moist air at 1 bar, $25^\circ C$, $\varphi_1=70\%$ is cooled to saturation, then post-heated to $20^\circ C$, $\varphi_3=40\%$. Find (1) cooling duty, (2) condensate rate, (3) post-heating duty. Tables: $p_s(25)=0.03169$, $p_s(20)=0.02339$ bar; $h_l(6^\circ C)\approx25.2$ kJ/kg.
exam.given: Process: 1→2 cool+dehumidify ($\varphi_2=100\%$), 2→3 heat at constant $\omega$. $R_a=287$ J/kgK.
exam.finalAnswer: $\dot Q_{cool}\approx-95.3$ kW, $\dot m_w\approx0.0196$ kg/s, $\dot Q_{heat}\approx33.9$ kW.
exam.tips: The post-heating leg fixes $\omega_2=\omega_3$, which then pins the saturated point 2. Cooling duty is negative (heat removed); heating positive.

exam.title: Conceptual: draw and explain the winter cycle
exam.statement: Describe the winter air-conditioning process on a psychrometric chart: starting from cold, humid outdoor air, reach the warm comfortable injection state I. Name each leg, state which property is constant, and write the total power requested.
exam.given: Comfort target winter: $t_I\approx21^\circ C$, $\varphi\approx50\%$. Mixing of return air E with fresh air R at point M.

steps.title: Mixing
steps.content: Return air (E) mixes with fresh outdoor air (R) → point M on segment R–E: $h_M=[(\dot m_{aI}-\dot m_{aR})h_E+\dot m_{aR}h_R]/\dot m_{aI}$.

steps.title: Pre-heating (M→1)
steps.content: Sensible heating at **constant $\omega$**: move right. Raises temperature so the air can then be humidified effectively.

steps.title: Humidification (1→2)
steps.content: Adiabatic water spray (air washer) at **constant $h$**: $\omega$ rises, $t$ falls slightly, until the desired moisture is reached.

steps.title: Post-heating (2→I)
steps.content: Sensible heating at constant $\omega$ up to the injection temperature $t_I$. Final state I has $\omega_I<\omega_E$ since the room is humidified by occupants ($\omega_E>\omega_I$).

steps.title: Power
steps.content: $\dot Q=\dot Q_t+\dot m_{aR}(h_E-h_R)$, where $\dot Q_t=\dot m_{aI}(h_I-h_E)$ compensates the room load and the second term brings fresh air to extraction conditions.

exam.finalAnswer: Legs: mix (R+E→M) → pre-heat (const ω) → humidify (const h) → post-heat (const ω) → inject at I. Total $\dot Q=\dot Q_t+\dot m_{aR}(h_E-h_R)$.
exam.tips: Pre-heat before humidifying so the spray can carry more water. Summer is the mirror image: cool+dehumidify (down the saturation curve) then post-heat.
